content.text: Rollback Runbooks for Claude Services

A rollback runbook is an executable, step-by-step procedure for reverting a specific Claude service to its last stable state. It is written before a release, not during an incident. During an incident, an engineer should be able to open the runbook, execute commands from top to bottom, and reach a stable state without making any judgment calls about what to do next.

A runbook that requires interpretation is not a runbook — it is notes. Write runbooks so that a qualified engineer who was not involved in the release can execute them end-to-end at 3am after being paged from sleep.

This sub-subtopic covers the rollback runbook structure, service-specific rollback procedures for the Claude API gateway, model-serving layer, configuration service, feature flags, database schema, and prompt templates, the time-to-recovery targets for each, and how to test runbooks before they are needed.

attrs.alt: Rollback execution flow (5 phases) and service-specific runbooks with TTR targets

content.text: Runbook Structure
Every Claude service rollback runbook follows the same section structure. Consistency matters: engineers switch between runbooks mid-incident and must orient immediately.
Section
Contents
Prerequisites
Required access (kubectl context, AWS role, LaunchDarkly access), tools that must be installed, who has permission to execute
Trigger criteria
Which alert or condition prompts this runbook — do not start the runbook unless the trigger is confirmed
Impact statement
What customers experience during the outage; what changes after rollback completes
Pre-rollback checklist
State to capture before executing (current revision, active flags, DB version) — needed for post-incident analysis
Rollback steps
Numbered commands with expected output after each step
Verification
Specific metrics/dashboards to confirm recovery; pass/fail criteria
Escalation
Who to page if rollback fails or verification does not pass within the TTR target
Post-rollback actions
Notification templates, incident record update, what must NOT be done until root cause is confirmed
API Gateway Rollback Runbook
The API gateway handles authentication, rate limiting, routing to model-serving backends, and response logging. A bad gateway release can affect all API traffic.
Trigger
Alert: RolloutLatencyP0 or RolloutErrorRateP0 fires on the gateway
On-call manually declares gateway rollback (e.g., after observing sustained error spike in dashboard)
Pre-Rollback State Capture

# Capture current state before rolling back
kubectl get rollout claude-gateway -n claude-api -o json \
  | jq '{name:.metadata.name, revision:.status.currentPodHash, stableRevision:.status.stableRS}' \
  > /tmp/pre-rollback-state.json

kubectl argo rollouts get rollout claude-gateway -n claude-api
# Note the current and stable revision numbers from output
Rollback Steps
Abort the in-progress rollout (reverts active service to stable ReplicaSet)
Verify rollback in progress
Confirm stable ReplicaSet is serving traffic
Run smoke test against production endpoint

# Step 1: Abort the rollout
kubectl argo rollouts abort claude-gateway -n claude-api
# Expected output: "rollout 'claude-gateway' aborted"

# Step 2: Watch rollout status (should reach 'Degraded' then 'Healthy')
kubectl argo rollouts status claude-gateway -n claude-api --timeout 120s
# Expected: "Healthy" within 60s

# Step 3: Confirm stable pods are serving
kubectl get pods -n claude-api -l app=claude-gateway --show-labels
# Expected: all pods with stable hash label; no canary pods remaining

# Step 4: Smoke test
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SMOKE_TEST_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022","max_tokens":10,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"hi"}]}' \
  https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages
# Expected: 200
Verification

# Check error rate returns to baseline (< 0.05%)
# In Grafana: open dashboard 'Claude API Gateway - Rollout Health'
# Panel: '5xx Error Rate by Version' — canary line should disappear

# PromQL check (run in Prometheus console):
rate(claude_http_errors_total{version="stable",status=~"5.."}[5m])
/ rate(claude_http_requests_total{version="stable"}[5m])
# Pass criterion: value < 0.0005 (0.05%)
Model-Serving Layer Rollback Runbook

The model-serving layer runs the Claude model inference pods. It is deployed via Helm. A rollback reverts the Helm release to the previous chart revision, which includes the previous model weights reference, replica count, and resource configuration.
Trigger
Eval score drops below 95% of baseline after a model weight update
P99 inference latency > 2× baseline for 5+ minutes
GPU OOM errors exceeding 0.1% of requests
Rollback Steps

# Step 1: Check current and previous Helm revisions
helm history claude-serving -n claude-serving --max 5
# Note: REVISION column — current is highest number; rollback target is current - 1

# Step 2: Execute Helm rollback
helm rollback claude-serving 0 -n claude-serving
# '0' means: roll back to the immediately previous revision
# Alternatively: helm rollback claude-serving <N> for a specific revision

# Step 3: Monitor rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/claude-serving -n claude-serving --timeout 180s
# Expected: "successfully rolled out" within 3 minutes

# Step 4: Verify model version
kubectl exec -n claude-serving deployment/claude-serving -- \
  env | grep MODEL_VERSION
# Expected: previous model version string

# Step 5: Run inference smoke test
python3 - <<'EOF'
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
msg = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
    max_tokens=32,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is 2+2?"}]
)
assert "4" in msg.content[0].text, f"Unexpected response: {msg.content[0].text}"
print("Smoke test passed")
EOF
Configuration Service Rollback Runbook

The configuration service distributes runtime config to all Claude API components: rate limit profiles, model routing rules, prompt template references, feature gate values. A bad config push can silently degrade all traffic without a code deployment.
Trigger
Metric divergence begins immediately after a config push with no code deployment
Feature gate evaluations returning unexpected values (observed in config audit log)
Rate limit profiles misapplied (customers hitting limits at wrong thresholds)
Rollback Steps

# Step 1: Identify the bad config commit
git log --oneline -10 infra/config/claude-api/

# Step 2: Revert the commit
git revert HEAD --no-edit   # or: git revert <bad-commit-sha> --no-edit
git push origin main

# Step 3: ArgoCD picks up the revert automatically (if auto-sync enabled)
# If manual sync required:
argocd app sync claude-api-config --prune
argocd app wait claude-api-config --health --timeout 120

# Step 4: Verify config is applied
kubectl get configmap claude-api-runtime-config -n claude-api -o yaml \
  | grep -A 5 "rate_limit"
# Expected: previous rate limit values

# Step 5: Check config service health
kubectl rollout status deployment/config-service -n claude-api --timeout 60s
Feature Flag Rollback Runbook

Feature flags are the fastest rollback path — no deployment required. For Claude features gated behind a flag, disabling the flag reverts behavior in under 30 seconds without touching Kubernetes or Helm.
Trigger
Metric regression observed after a flag was enabled for a percentage of traffic
Customer complaints correlated with flag enablement timestamp
Safety or quality issue observed in flagged-on cohort
Automated canary gate detects flag-on vs flag-off divergence
Rollback Steps
Database Schema Rollback Runbook

Database schema rollbacks are the most operationally complex. They require coordination between the application rollback and the schema rollback, and may require a maintenance window if the migration added NOT NULL constraints or dropped columns.

Never roll back a database schema migration without first confirming the application code has already been rolled back. Rolling back a schema while a new application version is serving traffic can cause immediate data corruption.
Pre-Conditions
Application (gateway + model-serving) has been rolled back to the previous version first
The migration script has a corresponding down migration that has been tested
A database backup taken within the last 15 minutes exists and is verified
DBA or engineer with write access to the production database is online
Rollback Steps

# Step 1: Verify application rollback is complete before touching DB
kubectl get pods -n claude-api -l app=claude-gateway \
  | grep -v "^NAME" | awk '{print $1}' \
  | xargs -I{} kubectl exec -n claude-api {} -- env | grep APP_VERSION
# All pods must show the PREVIOUS app version before proceeding

# Step 2: Take a fresh DB snapshot
aws rds create-db-snapshot \
  --db-instance-identifier claude-api-prod \
  --db-snapshot-identifier "pre-rollback-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"

# Step 3: Run the down migration
cd db/migrations
DATABASE_URL=$PROD_DB_URL alembic downgrade -1
# Expected output: "Running downgrade <migration-id> -> <previous-id>"

# Step 4: Verify schema
psql $PROD_DB_URL -c "\d request_logs"
# Confirm dropped columns are gone; old columns are present

# Step 5: Run application health check
curl -sf https://api.anthropic.com/health | jq .
Prompt Template Rollback Runbook

Claude system prompts and prompt templates are stored in S3 (or equivalent object storage) and fetched at request time by the gateway. Rolling back a prompt template does not require a deployment — it is a single S3 object revert.
Rollback Steps

# Step 1: List recent versions of the prompt template
aws s3api list-object-versions \
  --bucket claude-api-config-prod \
  --prefix prompts/system-prompt-v2.txt \
  | jq '[.Versions[] | {VersionId, LastModified, IsLatest}] | sort_by(.LastModified) | reverse | .[0:5]'

# Step 2: Identify the previous version ID from the list output
PREVIOUS_VERSION_ID="your-version-id-here"

# Step 3: Copy the previous version back as the current object
aws s3api copy-object \
  --bucket claude-api-config-prod \
  --copy-source "claude-api-config-prod/prompts/system-prompt-v2.txt?versionId=$PREVIOUS_VERSION_ID" \
  --key prompts/system-prompt-v2.txt \
  --metadata-directive COPY

# Step 4: Invalidate CDN cache (if prompt is cached at edge)
aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
  --distribution-id $CDN_DIST_ID \
  --paths "/prompts/*"

# Step 5: Verify active prompt content
aws s3 cp s3://claude-api-config-prod/prompts/system-prompt-v2.txt -
# Review output to confirm previous template is active
Testing Runbooks Before They Are Needed
A runbook that has never been executed is a hypothesis, not a procedure. Untested rollback procedures fail at the worst possible time.
Runbook Drill Schedule
Service
Drill Type
Frequency
Environment
API Gateway
Full rollback drill with live canary release
Monthly (before each major release)
Staging
Model Serving
Helm rollback drill
Quarterly
Staging
Config Service
Config revert drill (introduce bad config, revert)
Monthly
Staging
Feature Flags
Flag disable drill (enable then disable test flag)
Every release cycle
Production (test flag only)
Database
Down migration drill on production snapshot
Quarterly
Isolated production clone
Prompt Templates
S3 revert drill
Every release cycle
Production (non-customer-visible template)
Drill Checklist
Announce drill in #on-call-drills Slack channel with start/end time and scope
Execute runbook exactly as written — no shortcuts or 'I know what this does' skips
Record actual TTR vs. target TTR
Note any steps that were unclear, required googling, or produced unexpected output
Update the runbook with corrections before the drill is declared complete
File a Jira ticket for any tool access issues discovered (e.g., engineer did not have required IAM role)
Automated Runbook Validation

#!/bin/bash
# validate_runbook.sh — syntax-check runbook bash commands without executing
# Parses all code blocks from runbook markdown and runs 'bash -n' (syntax only)

RUNBOOK_FILE="$1"
if [ -z "$RUNBOOK_FILE" ]; then
  echo "Usage: $0 <runbook.md>"
  exit 1
fi

# Extract bash code blocks
python3 - <<EOF
import re, sys, subprocess, tempfile, os

with open('$RUNBOOK_FILE') as f:
    content = f.read()

blocks = re.findall(r'\`\`\`bash
(.*?)\`\`\`', content, re.DOTALL)
errors = []
for i, block in enumerate(blocks):
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', suffix='.sh', delete=False) as tf:
        tf.write(block)
        tf.flush()
        result = subprocess.run(['bash', '-n', tf.name], capture_output=True, text=True)
        if result.returncode != 0:
            errors.append(f"Block {i+1}: {result.stderr}")
        os.unlink(tf.name)

if errors:
    print("Syntax errors found:")
    for e in errors:
        print(f"  {e}")
    sys.exit(1)
else:
    print(f"All {len(blocks)} bash blocks syntax-valid.")
EOF
Common Rollback Failures and Prevention
Failure Mode
Root Cause
Prevention
Rollback command not found / permission denied
Engineer lacks kubectl context or IAM role
Verify access in pre-release drill; document required roles in Prerequisites section
Rollback succeeds but metrics don't recover
Root cause was not the deployed service (dependency failure, upstream issue)
Runbook verification step catches this; escalate if metrics don't recover within TTR
DB rollback causes data loss
Down migration drops data without backup
Require DB snapshot step before any schema rollback; test down migrations on production clone
Feature flag rollback takes > 5 min to propagate
SDK caching with long TTL on client side
Document cache TTL in runbook; include cache bust step; notify customers of expected delay
Helm rollback to wrong revision
'helm rollback 0' reverts to previous, not to a known-good revision
Always record target revision number in pre-rollback state capture; use explicit revision number
Runbook becomes stale after infrastructure changes
Runbooks not updated after kubectl context renames or namespace changes
Runbooks are code-reviewed artifacts; include runbook review in every infrastructure PR
Summary

Rollback runbooks are pre-written, executable procedures that an engineer can follow end-to-end during an incident without judgment calls. Each Claude service has a distinct rollback mechanism and TTR target: API gateway rollback via Argo Rollouts abort (< 60s), model-serving via Helm rollback (< 3m), config service via Git revert + ArgoCD (< 2m), feature flags via API toggle (< 30s), database via down migration with snapshot backup (< 30m), and prompt templates via S3 object revert (< 5m). Test runbooks in drills before they are needed — an untested runbook is a hypothesis. Update runbooks whenever the infrastructure changes, or they will fail exactly when you need them most.
Runbook sections: Prerequisites, Trigger criteria, Impact statement, Pre-rollback capture, Steps, Verification, Escalation, Post-rollback actions
Feature flag rollback is always fastest (< 30s); prefer flagging releases over deploying configuration to enable this path
Database schema rollbacks must always happen after (not before) application rollbacks
Drill monthly on staging; drill quarterly on production clones; record TTR vs target in each drill
Automated syntax validation of bash blocks prevents runbook rot
